category.label: FORMULAS
category.description: Interactive formula library — every key ratio and metric with variables, worked examples, and calculators.

entries.title: WACC
entries.subtitle: Weighted Average Cost of Capital — the blended required return on a firm's capital.
pitfalls: Using book-value weights instead of market-value weights — overstates debt cost for leveraged firms.
Ignoring the tax shield on debt — after-tax cost of debt = rate × (1 - tax rate).
Applying the same WACC to all divisions of a conglomerate — each business has its own risk profile.
Holding WACC constant across all projection years in a DCF — capital structure and rates evolve.

calculator.numerator_label: After-tax cost of debt × Debt weight + Cost of equity × Equity weight

entries.title: CAPM
entries.subtitle: Capital Asset Pricing Model — derives required equity return from beta and the equity risk premium.

actions.label: Open AAPL in Equity Research
actions.hint: Check beta and cost of equity

rules_of_thumb.label: Risk-free rate (10y UST)
rules_of_thumb.note: Use 10-year maturity to match investment horizon

pitfalls: Using the 3-month T-bill as Rf for a long-horizon DCF — match maturity to cash flow duration.
Taking beta from a 1-year daily regression — too noisy; use 3–5 year monthly betas.
Applying a single global ERP to emerging market equities without a country risk premium.
Confusing expected return (ex ante) with realized return (ex post).

entries.title: DCF
entries.subtitle: Discounted Cash Flow — intrinsic value as the present value of future free cash flows.

rules_of_thumb.label: Terminal value share of total
rules_of_thumb.note: Small changes in g or r dominate DCF value

rules_of_thumb.label: Terminal growth rate
rules_of_thumb.note: Should not exceed long-run nominal GDP growth

pitfalls: Terminal value often represents 70–80% of total DCF value — always run sensitivity tables on g and r.
Using nominal cash flows with a real discount rate (or vice versa).
Projecting FCF as a revenue percentage without ensuring capex and WC assumptions are consistent.
Setting terminal growth above long-run GDP — implies the company eventually dwarfs the global economy.

calculator.numerator_label: Sum of PV(FCF years 1–n) + PV(Terminal Value)

entries.title: Gordon Growth Model
entries.subtitle: Dividend Discount Model — stock price as the present value of growing perpetual dividends.

rules_of_thumb.label: Terminal growth rate (perpetuity)
rules_of_thumb.note: Must be less than discount rate r

rules_of_thumb.label: Sensitivity rule
rules_of_thumb.note: Halving the spread doubles the fair value

pitfalls: Setting g ≥ r — produces a negative or infinite result; meaningless.
Using current dividend yield (D/P) instead of forward dividend D₁ = D₀ × (1+g).
Applying to high-growth companies — constant-growth assumption is invalid when g is expected to change.
Ignoring share buybacks — companies returning cash via buybacks are undervalued by a pure DDM.

calculator.denominator_label: r − g (cost of equity minus growth rate)

entries.title: Sharpe Ratio
entries.subtitle: Risk-adjusted return per unit of total volatility — the universal fund comparison metric.

rules_of_thumb.label: Excellent
rules_of_thumb.note: Rare over multi-year periods in liquid markets

pitfalls: Comparing Sharpe ratios computed over different periods without annualizing consistently.
Not subtracting the risk-free rate — inflates Sharpe when rates are high.
Using Sharpe for non-normal return distributions (options selling, merger arb) — overstates risk-adjusted performance.
Interpreting a high Sharpe as 'safe' — large drawdowns can coexist with high Sharpe.

entries.title: Sortino Ratio
entries.subtitle: Risk-adjusted return penalizing only downside volatility — an improvement over Sharpe for asymmetric strategies.

rules_of_thumb.label: Acceptable
rules_of_thumb.note: Earns excess return per unit of downside risk

rules_of_thumb.label: Sortino vs Sharpe
rules_of_thumb.note: Sortino is always ≥ Sharpe for the same portfolio

pitfalls: Using total volatility instead of downside deviation — that is Sharpe, not Sortino.
Setting the target return to zero when a meaningful hurdle exists.
Comparing Sortinos computed with different target returns.
Over-relying on Sortino for short return histories — downside deviation is noisy with few observations.

entries.title: Information Ratio
entries.subtitle: Active return per unit of tracking error — the primary measure of active management consistency.

rules_of_thumb.label: Weak
rules_of_thumb.note: Very few active managers sustain this

rules_of_thumb.label: Acceptable
rules_of_thumb.note: Median active fund over full cycles

pitfalls: High IR from low tracking error — hugging the benchmark can produce a respectable IR on tiny active return.
Using a mismatched benchmark — an equity fund benchmarked to a bond index produces meaningless IR.
Treating IR as stationary — it degrades if the manager's edge becomes crowded.
Annualizing tracking error incorrectly — multiply monthly TE by sqrt(12) only when returns are approximately independent.

entries.title: Treynor Ratio
entries.subtitle: Excess return per unit of systematic risk (beta) — appropriate for funds held within a diversified portfolio.

rules_of_thumb.label: Higher is better
rules_of_thumb.note: Absolute level less meaningful than relative ranking

pitfalls: Using Treynor for a standalone investment — total volatility matters; use Sharpe instead.
Using beta estimated over too short a period — 60-month betas are standard.
Comparing Treynor across funds benchmarked to different indices.
A positive Treynor in a bear market may simply reflect low beta, not skill.

entries.title: Kelly Criterion
entries.subtitle: Optimal fraction of capital to risk per bet to maximize long-run compounded wealth.

rules_of_thumb.label: Practical usage
rules_of_thumb.note: Reduces variance; accounts for edge estimation error

pitfalls: Treating estimated probabilities as exact — Kelly is very sensitive to p and b inputs.
Using full Kelly in leveraged or illiquid instruments — produces terrifying drawdowns.
Ignoring correlation across simultaneous positions — multi-asset Kelly requires a covariance matrix.
Applying Kelly to strategies with non-stationary edge.

calculator.numerator_label: b × p − q  (net odds × win prob − lose prob)

entries.title: Parametric VaR
entries.subtitle: Value at Risk — maximum likely loss over a horizon at a given confidence level, assuming normal returns.
pitfalls: Treating VaR as the maximum possible loss — it is a percentile threshold, not a ceiling.
Using parametric VaR for options-heavy portfolios — normal distribution assumption breaks down.
Failing to account for correlation breakdown in stress periods.
Annualizing daily VaR by multiplying by 252 instead of sqrt(252).

entries.title: CVaR / Expected Shortfall
entries.subtitle: Average loss in the tail beyond VaR — answers how bad it gets when VaR is breached.

rules_of_thumb.label: Basel IV metric
rules_of_thumb.note: Replaced 99% VaR for market risk capital

pitfalls: Confusing CVaR with 'the worst-case loss' — it is the average of the tail, not an absolute worst case.
Estimating CVaR from short return histories — the tail is defined by few observations; very noisy.
Ignoring the distinction between parametric and historical CVaR during stress periods.
Treating CVaR as complete without scenario analysis — black-swan events may not appear in any sample.

calculator.denominator_label: Not applicable (CVaR is a mean, not a ratio)

entries.title: Maximum Drawdown
entries.subtitle: Largest peak-to-trough portfolio decline over a period — the most intuitive measure of downside risk.

rules_of_thumb.label: Calmar ratio target
rules_of_thumb.note: Annualized return exceeds max drawdown magnitude

pitfalls: Measuring MDD over a short history that excludes known crisis periods.
Treating MDD as complete downside risk without considering CVaR or scenario analysis.
Comparing MDD across strategies with different time periods.
Ignoring time to recovery — depth and duration are both relevant.

entries.title: P/E Formula
entries.subtitle: Price-to-Earnings — how much investors pay per dollar of earnings, with full worked example.

rules_of_thumb.label: Negative EPS
rules_of_thumb.note: P/E is meaningless when earnings are negative

pitfalls: Comparing P/E across sectors without context (banks vs SaaS — useless).
Using trailing P/E at cyclical troughs — EPS is depressed; the stock looks expensive when it may not be.
Ignoring leverage — two companies with the same P/E can have very different risk profiles.
Trusting adjusted EPS without checking what management excluded.

entries.title: EV/EBITDA Formula
entries.subtitle: Enterprise value as a multiple of operating earnings — compares companies across capital structures.

rules_of_thumb.label: Capital-intensive warning
rules_of_thumb.note: EBITDA overstates FCF for high-capex businesses

pitfalls: Treating EBITDA as a cash flow proxy for capex-heavy businesses — a pipeline company with $500 mn EBITDA and $450 mn capex has almost no FCF.
Not using market-value debt — if bonds trade at a discount, using face value overstates debt in EV.
Including operating lease debt but not adjusting EBITDA — stay consistent post-IFRS 16/ASC 842.
Comparing across GAAP and IFRS reporters without adjustment.

calculator.numerator_label: Enterprise Value ($mn) — Market Cap + Debt − Cash

entries.title: PEG Ratio
entries.subtitle: P/E adjusted for expected growth — penalizes expensive multiples without the earnings growth to justify them.
pitfalls: Using a single-year growth estimate instead of a multi-year CAGR.
Applying PEG to mature, low-growth companies — not meaningful below ~10% EPS growth.
Taking analyst consensus growth forecasts at face value — systematically optimistic.
Ignoring quality — two stocks with identical PEGs can have very different return profiles.

entries.title: R-Squared
entries.subtitle: Coefficient of determination — the fraction of portfolio variance explained by the benchmark.

rules_of_thumb.label: Closet indexer warning
rules_of_thumb.note: Fund moves nearly in lockstep with benchmark

pitfalls: High R² does not imply good performance — a closet indexer underperforming by 1% annually can have R² ≈ 0.99.
Using R² to infer causation — correlation is not causation.
Computing R² over too short a period — 36–60 months is standard.
R² is always non-negative — it cannot tell you the direction of the relationship.

entries.title: Beta Formula
entries.subtitle: Systematic risk measure — how much a stock moves per 1% move in the market.
pitfalls: Using daily returns for beta estimation — microstructure noise biases beta downward; use monthly.
Not adjusting for dividends — use total return series.
Assuming beta is stable — it changes with leverage, business mix, and market regime.
Applying historical beta to a company that underwent major transformation.

entries.title: Duration
entries.subtitle: Macaulay and Modified Duration — bond price sensitivity to interest rate changes.
pitfalls: Confusing Macaulay Duration (time measure) with Modified Duration (price sensitivity) — they are related but different.
Ignoring convexity for large rate moves — duration is a linear approximation.
Using Modified Duration for callable or puttable bonds — use Effective Duration instead.
Mismatching the yield convention — Macaulay ÷ (1 + y/m) must match coupon frequency.

entries.title: Black-Scholes
entries.subtitle: Closed-form European option pricing model — the foundational framework for all options valuation.

rules_of_thumb.label: Theta decay accelerates
rules_of_thumb.note: Time decay is fastest near expiry

rules_of_thumb.label: Vega peaks at ATM
rules_of_thumb.note: ATM options most sensitive to vol changes

pitfalls: Treating Black-Scholes price as the 'true' price — normal distribution assumption underestimates tail risk.
Ignoring the volatility smile/skew — implied vol is not constant across strikes.
Using historical volatility instead of implied volatility for pricing.
Applying to American options without adjustment — Black-Scholes prices European options only.

entries.title: Put-Call Parity
entries.subtitle: No-arbitrage link between European call and put prices — C − P = S − PV(K).

actions.label: Open AAPL in Equity Research
actions.hint: View options chain for parity check

pitfalls: Applying put-call parity to American options as if it were an equality.
Forgetting dividends — discrete dividends before expiry break the parity formula.
Treating parity deviations as vol signals — deviations in liquid markets are almost always explained by dividends, borrow, or bid-ask spreads.
Mixing discrete and continuous discounting conventions in the same formula.

entries.title: Compound Interest
entries.subtitle: FV = PV × (1 + r)^n — the foundational time-value formula underlying all of finance.
pitfalls: Confusing nominal rate with the periodic rate — 12% annual compounded monthly uses r = 1% per month.
Confusing real and nominal returns — compound nominal at nominal rate; real wealth at real return.
Treating compound interest as additive — mentally adding returns year by year understates terminal wealth.
Ignoring tax drag — taxes on dividends and realized gains each year reduce the effective compounding rate.

entries.title: CAGR
entries.subtitle: Compound Annual Growth Rate — the smoothed annual return rate that equates start value to end value over n years.
pitfalls: Using CAGR to imply smoothness — a fund with 9% CAGR could have had years of −40%.
Comparing CAGRs over different periods without adjusting for starting conditions.
Annualizing returns from less than 1 year of data — introduces extreme noise.
Ignoring reinvestment assumption — CAGR assumes all cash flows reinvested at the same rate.

calculator.denominator_label: Raise to power (1/n years), then subtract 1

entries.title: Position Sizing
entries.subtitle: Kelly / fixed fractional / R-multiple — how much capital to risk per trade.

rules_of_thumb.label: Kelly in practice
rules_of_thumb.note: Use 50% of Kelly fraction to reduce variance

pitfalls: Setting stop too tight to avoid a large position — stop must be at a technically meaningful level, not sized backwards.
Ignoring correlated positions — two 1% risk positions with 0.9 correlation effectively double the risk.
Using the same risk % regardless of setup quality — scale risk with conviction.
Failing to reduce size during drawdown — Kelly naturally scales down; never override by trading bigger.

entries.title: ROE Formula
entries.subtitle: Return on Equity — net income as a percentage of shareholders' equity, with DuPont decomposition.
pitfalls: High ROE from high leverage is not the same as high ROE from high margins — use DuPont to distinguish.
Negative equity (after aggressive buybacks) makes ROE meaningless — use ROIC instead.
Comparing ROE across sectors without adjustment.
Not averaging equity — using ending equity overstates ROE when equity grew significantly.

entries.title: Free Cash Flow Formula
entries.subtitle: FCF = Operating Cash Flow − CapEx — the cash available after funding operations and reinvestment.
aliases: cash flow from operations minus capex
pitfalls: Using reported CapEx without distinguishing maintenance from growth capex — understates true free cash generation.
Ignoring lease payments under IFRS 16 / ASC 842 — distorts CFO comparisons across periods.
Treating short-term negative FCF as terminal — capex-heavy growth phases temporarily depress FCF.
Comparing FCF without adjusting for working capital seasonality.
